summary: Group membership is now sticky against automatic tab-lifecycle removal: Chrome's native ungroup, a navigation-eject, and MV3 restart flicker ungroup the tab visually but no longer delete a recorded member URL. A member leaves a group only through an explicit user action or a genuine re-home (A→B).

changes.title: Sticky membership when a tab leaves all groups
changes.description: The onUpdated handler's groupId → -1 branch no longer deletes the member urlKey (and no longer records a WORKER_TAB_UNGROUPED removal). A tab leaving all groups — Chrome's native ungroup gesture, a navigation-eject, or MV3 restart flicker — is still marked ungrouped in activeTabs, but its label membership survives. This resolves the contradiction with groupTabs, which already treats members as sticky by auto-regrouping a matching tab back into its label. Reopening the URL auto-regroups it.

waysToTest.text: Read the worker tests in service_worker.test.js — describe blocks 'onUpdated group leave keeps member sticky' and the navigation-eject case in 'onUpdated in-page URL change keeps tab grouped'.

tests.name: onUpdated group leave keeps member sticky › keeps the member when a tab leaves a group
tests.description: When a grouped tab is ungrouped (groupId → -1), its urlKey remains a member of the label instead of being spliced out. Red if the old ungroup splice were restored.

tests.name: onUpdated group leave keeps member sticky › marks the tab ungrouped in activeTabs without touching labels
tests.description: The ungroup path still writes activeTabs with the tab's groupId set to -1 (bookkeeping preserved) but never writes labels, proving membership is left intact.

tests.name: onUpdated in-page URL change keeps tab grouped › keeps the member when a navigation ejects a grouped tab
tests.description: A real navigation still calls chrome.tabs.ungroup to eject the tab visually, but the recorded member survives — the eject is not an explicit removal.

changes.title: Re-home (A→B) still removes from the old group
changes.description: A tab moved directly from group A into group B is unambiguous re-parent intent, so handleActiveTabsGroupChanges still adds the member to B and removes it from A. This removal is reached only when a real destination group is present (the pre-existing !newGroup guard already skips ungroup-to-nothing), now documented explicitly so the sticky contract is legible at the splice site. This keeps a URL from accumulating membership in every group it ever passed through.

waysToTest.text: Read the 'handleActiveTabsGroupChanges' describe block in service_worker.test.js — the re-home and ungroup-to-nothing cases.

tests.name: handleActiveTabsGroupChanges › re-home A→B adds the member to B and removes it from A
tests.description: Moving a tab from group A directly into group B records the member in B and splices it out of A — the sanctioned re-parent path.

tests.name: handleActiveTabsGroupChanges › ungroup-to-nothing keeps the member in the old group
tests.description: When newGroup is absent (getTabGroup(-1) resolves null), the diff body is skipped and the old group keeps its member — membership stays sticky, distinguishing an ungroup from a re-home.
